sentences.text: It began at von Buchenwald, because as I mentioned before, we were going from Ohrdruf to Buchenwald.
And we arrived in Buchenwald, but they didn't have where to put us.
They-- the camp was over-- they had more people they could handle.
And I remember I was sleeping and almost in the water, because they just didn't have where to put us.
And they didn't have trains anymore.
Because all the trains-- I imagine, that my guess that they were-- they needed in the front to evacuate.
And so they decided to send us somewhere.
And they just sent us to Flossenburg.
And that was a pretty far-- I mean, to walk for--
I don't know exactly how far, but it was a few hundred kilometer, I would say.
But I really don't know.
I don't remember.
You know, it seems so far when you're walking, and you-- you didn't have what to eat, and you could hardly make a step.
So--
We, I believe-- of course, I was not privy-- privy to-- to all those information at that time.
But I-- I believe it was 1,600 people.
And there were a lot of guards, a lot of SS guards with us.
And we were walking.
And in the beginning, it was not too bad because you still had a little bit of strength.
But as the days were going-- I mean, after a few days, everybody was exhausted and didn't get any food.
And sometimes we stop in some kind of a-- with a farmer and just sleep over once in two nights, I imagine-- or three nights.
I don't remember exactly.
Sometimes we slept on the-- on the ground.
And once in a farm, we found some-- some grains.
Some raw potatoes.
And in the morning, usually-- usually they-- when they found somebody has grains in the pocket, they would get a lot of beating, you know.
And it happened to me that I got beating on the way back when they find that I have some grain in the thing because we were stealing.
We were there-- we-- the farmers showed us the hospitality, and they were stealing from them.
So we got beating for it.
But that's the only way you could survive.
And whoever couldn't walk was just shot.
And all mostly it was taking-- we had a wagon, and the prisoners was pulling the wagon.
And in the evening, they took-- took those people who couldn't walk to the to-- to-- the woods.
When we're passing whenever they found some woods, and they shot them.
Or when people were running away from the-- from the transport during the day, so they shot them on the spot.
There were some people who escaped and survived somehow, you know.
But-- but most of them, they got killed.
They got.
And I remember when I had a very good friend, and-- which I escaped later together.
And--
Oh, Schreiber.
Schreiber.
We escaped, and he survived, too.
But he died a few a months-- a few years ago.
And we had another friend.
And we were with me and my friend were pulling him, pulling him.
And he was a man a head taller than we were.
Bohanek.
Henech Bohanek.
This name I remember.
Henech Bohanek.
And we were--
He was from Krakow.
And we were pulling him, and we're pulling him.
And then the guard saw that we were pulling him, and that they were slowing the-- the transport, you know.
And they took him in the-- in the wagon, and he was shot in the evening when wherever we stop.
So whoever couldn't-- there were many people on the road you could see they shot.
I imagine there were all kind of transport.
We were not the only one.
And by the time we-- it got to-- to Flossenburg-- because that's where we were supposed to go originally.
And we were-- we were camping outside of Flossenburg, and they-- they-- they wouldn't accept us, because they were evacuating themselves.
And they were-- and we were supposed to go.
The guards told us that we are going to Dachau, which was quite a long way yet to go.
So I knew at that time, I knew couldn't make it, because I was very, very, weak already.
And I and this, my friend Schreiber, somehow we-- we-- overnight, we slept in a-- in a school.
An old school, in a village school somewhere.
And we investigated.
We went down to the cellar.
We found a window in the cellar, which was the [INAUDIBLE] outside of the building.
And at night, we-- three people, actually.
Another one who was just a lucky boy-- we escaped.
And we heard some shooting later.
And people-- I found out who survived, they said that they were looking for us.
Yes.
But then they had to go, and we were-- we were in the woods.
There was-- and we escaped at night.
And we were walking at night.
We wanted to go as far as we can go.
Naturally, they had to go farther.
And there we were in the woods.
Let's see, the second of August, it was my-- second of April was my birthday.
I remember the guard told me that my uncle died.
Roosevelt was my uncle.
And so that was about a few days later that we escaped, about three or four days later that must have been.
And I was liberated, I believe-- I don't know exactly what day I was liberated.
I was about 12, 13 days in the woods.
Every time we found a place and-- and we got [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to the-- they had kept chickens, for instance.
They had this very small place where they kept the chicken.
And they had a little bit of hay, and we went.
We got inside.
And-- and in the morning, the woman came in to look for the eggs, and they found us.
And so she got scared.
And then we were supposed to-- you just go further.
We just-- and she'd actually-- they all-- they all got scared that we were the prisoners.
And they didn't know.
I imagine they were scared of the Germans.
They were scared of us.
So we were going down by-- by-- by night.
We were walking.
And in the morning, usually they-- they caught us somehow.
And once when we went there, we stopped and they were where the forester had a house.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was a Pole, I remember.
Oh, he said, he's going to help us.
He's going to help us.
Don't worry.
Everything is all right.
You are safe here.
You are safe here.
All of a sudden, he disappeared.
And then we were suspicious.
And then we went to the-- to the-- didn't want to stay in the-- in the barrack.
And we came and he came with a Germans somehow.
So I don't know what happened, because one of the Czech boy-- man-- was already sick.
And he-- we met him in a hospital, so somehow he survived.
But we were scared.
I don't know what happened with the Czech guy, if they-- if he brought SS people or not.
But we were scared.
We didn't know.
Somehow, we were-- we were always going further, further, and farther.
And--
At that time?
Not to-- to help.
I mean, they help us when they found us that we were there, and they gave us something to eat.
But it's just to go away.
I mean, they didn't want nothing to do with us.
But they were scared themselves.
But to help, I don't think, no, no.
We didn't have any occasion, because we were always on the run.
And I don't know the intention of this Polish guy who was there, because I think that he brought some Germans, because we saw it.
[INAUDIBLE] he brought somebody there.
But we didn't stay long enough to-- to-- to know who it was.
And finally, we-- we went to a place, I remember.
We took off the jackets, because not to be recognized as the-- that we were prisoners.
And I remember, we went to eat something somewhere.
And a guy was looking at us, and they were looking at-- And I thought that-- you know, at the time, all the guards were changing in civilian suits.
And they were always holding something under their arm.
And I-- somehow we recognized that he must have been a guard.
And I remember, there was a public place where they ate during.
And we saw them, so we went out.
And he went after us.
And so he shot something.
But he-- you know, we thought he didn't know what to do.
He knew it's end of the world.
He knew that he was afraid like for his safety, and he didn't know if his duty was to kill us or to save himself.
That's how we felt.
So we went to the woods again.
And-- and after that, in 12 or 13 days, we saw the American army coming in and the-- and the Jeeps.
And so we started to talk to them or what.
We didn't know English.
But we started to somehow we gave signs.
And so they were very all business.
Stay on this side and that's it.
They didn't.
And then we came to a first small town once Americans were there.
We [INAUDIBLE] a small town of Eslarn, I remember, which was east of [GERMAN].
And we went-- we went to first house and said-- that because we were too dirty.
We had louse and all kind of lice on us from-- as I said, we were [INAUDIBLE] at that time.
And first, we took the bath.
And the women were there scared, because the men were all on front of wherever they were.
And-- and we finally we took the first time in bath.
We didn't sleep for quite a long time.
I thought that I was falling in somewhere.
And I took a bath and washed ourselves.
And next morning, she told us that's the registration.
They went in, and we stayed in line.
And everybody was looking at us, because we were strange faces.
And then we--
That was in Germany.
Everything in Germany, Bavarian.
Eslarn.
And we came in to the front, and I told American soldiers, who are you?
So we told them the story.
Oh, they closed up the whole thing.
And he said, why do you stay in line?
We said, what do you mean?
They said, line.
They told us stay in line.
We stayed in line.
And they took us to the-- to the burgermeister, which is like a mayor.
And they told us to give us somewhere to sleep or something.
So he gave us somewhere.
And there was small town outskirts.
It was like a bar tavern.
And they had the rooms upstairs, so we were there in the room.
And-- and then-- then the-- the same soldiers went down.
It was the Patton 5th Division, I believe.
They went down Czechoslovakia.
And new people came in, you know.
Every day, somebody else.
But we were assigned there, so we had a room.
In the morning, they gave us something to eat.
Then my-- my friend got sick.
He didn't feel good.
And they didn't have a hospital.
It was a small place, so they had a school.
And the nuns were there.
And they were running the first aid.
So he stayed there in the-- in the-- in the school.
And after that, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] one morning, after a day or two, I went down.
And there were young kids.
You know, American kids in the army.
And they-- they already fraternized with the girls.
And-- and-- and they were in the bar, and they're getting drinks, and this and that.
So they-- they started to ask questions.
Everybody wanted to be a hero.
So we started question the Germans.
Who are you?
Oh, they start saying, we don't know.
So they started to talk to us.
Who are you?
And we said, [INAUDIBLE] is a Jewish fellow.
He's from concentration.
Lager, he said.
I didn't know camp at that time.
And-- and they didn't know what to do.
They wanted so-- they-- what language you speak?
I said, I know Jewish.
I know Polish.
But they didn't know.
So they took us to the road-- took me to the road.
And they had a convoy.
And they stopped the convoy, and they asked, who knows Polish?
And somebody said, yeah, I know Polish.
And he-- I started to talk Polish, but he didn't-- he didn't know really any Polish.
So I said, does he speak Polish?
I said, sort of.
He said he didn't know Polish.
They still didn't know what to do.
They-- they put us so stop at a PM and MP.
And they put it on the car, on the Jeep, and they took us somewhere.
And I saw a trunk-load of German prisoners of war.
They took us to camp-- took me to camp.
And they started to make their all kind of up, down, this, with the Germans, because there were in uniform.
And I was standing on the side.
So I said, listen.
Then he comes to me.
He says, well, who are you?
So I said, and I told him, I am a-- from the concentration camp.
And-- and so he said, so what are you doing here?
I said, I don't know.
They took me in, [LAUGHS] because they didn't know what to do with me.
Anyway, he couldn't let me out, because I was-- I was counted.
And finally at 12 o'clock, he talked to somebody, and they let me go.
And so the next day, I figure, I'm not going to stay here.
I went to the-- to the school.
And I said, I am sick.
And-- and a few days later, two days later, they took us to hospital in Weiden.
And then we spent a few weeks in Weiden.
In the hospital, yes.
And then after a few weeks, after my friend was feeling better, we went to Bayreuth.
And that's where we were staying after the war for-- for about a year.
In the beginning, we had only 13 people.
Then people started to come in, come in, come in.
And there was a Jewish chaplain, and he-- he-- rabbi.
And he sort of organized a [NON-ENGLISH] for the people who are-- who were there.
And you know, everybody was looking for somebody.
Everybody thought that he was the only one who survived.
And eventually, there were about 300 people.
And they thought they were the [NON-ENGLISH] and-- no.
And then I was a [NON-ENGLISH].
I was one of the five who was elected to be as a representative of the Jewish people in Bayreuth.
And then we were getting some packages from UNRRA and that's-- that's what it is.
And a lot of people were getting married.
They found each other.
And we were going to the weddings every week.
[LAUGHS] And then I-- I got-- when I was in the hospital in Weiden, I met some-- some soldiers.
And I asked them if they can-- I didn't remember the others.
I knew that I have a brother in the United States, and he was a doctor.
And-- but I-- the last others I remember, he was in the Bronx somewhere.
But meanwhile, he was in the army.
He was in Europe.
He was in Italy at that time.
So my-- and then I told him that I have a uncles in Staten Island.
But I gave them the name of my brother, I believe.
Somehow they-- they got-- there was another story.
They told me how they got to my uncles, because my brother was in Europe and was in the army.
And so he was not-- they couldn't locate him.
And somehow, they found out that he had a family in Staten Island.
And-- and they sent me affidavit.
I met somebody that they sent where-- and he wrote them.
Where-- I mean-- I met-- he was a captain in the army from Staten Island, and we had the address.
And they send me the affidavit.
And meanwhile, my brother, they told me, he wrote me a letter from Italy.
That-- they wrote him, to my brother, that I am in Germany.
And he was at Italy.
And so-- and he got my address.
I don't remember if he sent me a letter or not.
If I got the letter or he didn't have time to.
But he asked for furlough to be able to come and see me.
But meanwhile, there was still war going on in the far east, and he was sent in to Japan.
So by the time when he got the answer that he can't, be on account of the condition in Europe and in the far east that he can't get the furlough, he was in Japan anyway already by that time.
So I got the affidavit from my uncles.
And then one month before I arrived to United States, I got the affidavit from my-- my brother, who came back already.
He came one month before I arrived to United States.
So I came actually on his affidavit.
I got the-- no.
Through Bremerhaven.
I had to go to consulate in Munich at that time.
And then they send-- I was actually in second transport to United States on a small boat.
You know, those army boats. 8,000 whatever.
And I was sick on the boat.
And arrived the 15th of July to-- in 1946 to the United States.
About-- I believe it was about six years ago.
We went with Jenny, my wife, this wife.
We went to Europe.
We went to Austria.
And her sister and her husband and-- four people.
So we went to Austria.
And then we went to-- I rented a car and went to Hungary.
And my brother-in-law had somebody, family in [NON-ENGLISH], outside of [NON-ENGLISH], eastern Czechoslovakia.
Then we stopped for four days to Krakow.
And I went to my Tarnow.
And I went from Krakow.
We drove from Krakow to Tarnow.
And the town was completely changed.
After 50 years or 40-- whatever, 45 at that time-- everything was changed.
The-- the-- the-- the places that I-- when I saw, I remember there were new houses, which were old already.
And I was very disappointed.
I wanted to see the cemetery where my mother, who died in 1941, during the war.
And she didn't have a real.
At that time, it was just before the ghetto.
And-- and when she died, my father said, listen, we don't know what's going to be tomorrow.
And you can't wait for the-- for the real stone to put up.
So here we put in a small foot stone like a temporary.
He said, in case everything will be all right, we'll put down a stone.
I couldn't find it.
The half of cemetery was made new road.
And I was very disappointed.
I was actually only a few hours there.
I went with somebody that used to live at the same house, the big house.
And-- but I was very disappointed somehow.
I-- Maybe I wish-- I planning to go sometimes and take most of time.
And I remember those places.
But I know I'm going to be disappointed, too, somehow.
It's-- it's not the same anymore.
You know, you dream about coming to America.
And when you come, the dream somehow disappear.
I mean, the reality is not what you dream.
And it was very hard.
I didn't have any-- any-- any trade.
I was working as a-- as a dental technician during the war for my brother-in-law.
But here, they-- somehow, by that time, I was getting $12 a week.
I couldn't live on it.
And my brother was single at that time.
And we were living together in one room.
And then he got himself a-- he came in in '46.
And all the doctors came out from the war.
And there was too many doctors, all of a sudden.
And they sent.
So he was sent somewhere in the outside of Rochester as a-- as a farm doctor in the farm.
A small place.
And he was struggling, and I was struggling.
And he decided to go and specialize, later on.
Three years later.
And I-- then I had some kind of second or third uncle.
And I got a job where I was getting $28.
And I was-- I wanted to learn a trade.
But somehow, he didn't-- you know, he-- they didn't give me a chance.
He said he gave somebody a chance to learn the trade.
And then when he learned, he left him.
So he didn't want to do it anymore.
So I left him.
And I got the job in the store.
I remember Broad Street, which was also paying $28.
That was my big job.
They were my big jobs.
And somehow, I never could get-- being that I-- I didn't have a trade, I had to struggle.
Then I was married in 1949.
How did you meet your wife?
I-- [LAUGHS] strangely enough, I had a girlfriend after the war in Europe.
And-- and we were-- but I-- she had a father who went-- he was also a survivor.
He went to Israel, at that time, Palestine.
And she had a sister, and she wanted to go to the father.
And I wanted to go to America.
And we were very much in love, but somehow not enough in love to-- to-- to sacrifice each other.
And so she said-- but we were corresponding.
She sent me letters and love letters here and there, too, you know.
And then once, she said, you know, we have a friend.
I have a friend in New York, and they live here.
And he also survived, who came out at the same time that I did.
And they were somehow a family that it was almost intact.
And they-- they rented a small house, and everybody was working.
And there were a lot of boys again.
There were a lot of girls then.
A lot of boys were getting there.
It was like a-- like a place that you get together.
And I met my wife over there.
And then after-- after all of those letters, we decided-- she was supposed-- the other one from Israel was supposed to come to United States, but it never materialized.
And-- and I-- and I met meanwhile my wife, and we got married '49.
Sonia.
Her maiden name was Bruschtein.
She was from Krakow.
And she was, I believe, was family of four also.
And she lost all her family.
And she was the only one who was very Jewish looking.
So the family decided to let her go on errant papers and send her to friends, to Warsaw somewhere.
But meanwhile, as she was shook whenever they-- they-- they found her.
Some kind they wanted money that she's Jewish, shaking up with money.
And as long as she had money, she was giving.
But later, she didn't have money, so they-- she was denounced.
And she put-- she was put on the train to Treblinka.
And she jumped the train.
And she was all blooded from the-- from when she jumped.
And she came back to-- to Warsaw.
She went right to the arbeitsamt which was workplace, when they were sending people to Germany-- the Poles.
So she said she's volunteering to go to Germany.
And she wants to escape from the family who are very, very Polish.
And she's pro-- pro-German.
And so that's how she got into Germany as a-- as a Polish girl.
And she was working for a German family.
And then she-- she-- then she changed places.
And she went to another German family, which were very prominent.
An engineer who was working on the V1 and V2, which was sent to England.
You know, like missiles.
Missiles.
The first missiles actually were developed by Germans, and they were the one that she was-- she was serving the old big shots of Germany hierarchy in their home.
But she-- he was a Nazi, but he loved her, because she-- they didn't have children.
And she was very nice, and she spoke German.
And she was saying that she was a pro-German, and she was a this, and she had to-- because it was very strange that she didn't have any correspondence with her family.
So how was she going to explain this?
So she said she escaped because they are all very nationalistic Pollocks, and she was pro-German, and she didn't-- and so they didn't want to hear nothing from her.
And that's how she survived.
And then, she was denounced by some, by Polish people somewhere recognized her.
But the guy had to-- to give Nazi words that she is not Jewish.
Yes.
We'll continue on the next tape.
